speaker: interviewer
text: Today is August 8, 1995. This is an interview about the survivor Dr. Otto Schneid, S-C-H-N-E-I-D. This is an interview with Miriam Schneid-Ofseyer, S-C-H-N-E-I-D-O-F-S-E-Y-E-R. The interviewer is Rosanne Krusner, K-R-U-S-N-E-R. We're in Toronto, Ontario, Canada. And the language of the interview is in English. Today is August 8, 1995. This is an interview about Dr. Otto Schneid. It is an interview with Miriam Schneid-Ofseyer. I'm the interviewer, Rosanne Krusner. And we're in Toronto, Ontario, Canada. And the language of the interview is in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: What we are seeing now is a sculpture by the late Otto Schneid. It is in bronze. The original is made of stone, and it is in the Pitti Palace in Florence, Firenze. And it's a self-portrait, with a huge bird. When I asked Otto, what is this bird, why is it pecking down your forehead, he said, it could be one of two things. It's either inspiration, or it's, perhaps, death. While thinking about the question of that, I was interested to find out if there was any connection between the eagle and the Germans. And Rosanne told me that on the German flag, there is the iron eagle. So the one that is pecking on him are the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please introduce yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Miriam Schneid-Ofseyer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in a little shtetl near Minsk in Russia called Lahoysk.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1924, the eight candle of Hanukkah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you live in Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Uh, my family stayed in Russia till I was 2 and 1/2 years old. And then my father went to Israel and settled in, unfortunately, in the worst place in Hebron, which is even now in the news. And, uh, there, he stayed several years, till the pogrom of 1929 came. And when the hour of the mob of Arabs entered our house and they went from house to house, killing, I tried to save my father. They dragged him outside. And I tried to save him. So one of them used an ax and split my head. Uh, my mother survived. She was also wounded on her nose. But she pretended to be dead. And an Arab was standing above her and watching her. And when everything was quiet and she opened her eyes to make sure that everything is OK, when nothing was OK, the Arab slashed her belly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I came-- 5 years old-- when I came to my mother to say, look, Dad is there in the veranda, lying. His eyes are open. He doesn't get up. She said to me, give him some water.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I took a glass of water. Actually, it was, you know, this thing that you are doing-- [INAUDIBLE] that you wash your hands. And it was very heavy. And I brought it to my father, and I asked him to drink. But he was, of course, dead. He couldn't do anything. And I was lost. I didn't know what to do. So I poured the water on his face. And, uh, afterwards, for many, many years, I blamed myself that I did not understand my mother correctly. Were I to understand her and really give him water, perhaps he would not die.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then from Hebron, we went to Jerusalem. And there, I studied in a religious school and got all the education. And I went up to the teacher's seminary and finished being a teacher. And all the rest happened in [NON-ENGLISH], the main thing of youth-- main things of youth. And you became a poet.

speaker: interviewee
text: I became a poet when I was 8. I started to write when I was 8. And, uh, I remember now, my little friends-- little girl friends-- would ask me, why don't you want to kill all the Arabs?

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, to kill all the Arabs? There are criminals among them. If I were to find the man who killed my father, I would perhaps kill him, too. But just to kill all the Arabs? They are victims, as I was. No, you don't kill all the Arabs. And I seemed very strange to them. They did not understand what I was talking.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I started to write at the age of 8 because between 5 and 8, I was probably in the hospitals and all that to-- to gain my-- my strength and health.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just so that we have an idea of the kind of poetry that you write, can you recite something for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Yeah, it will be a pleasure. I will recite a poem that was a dream. It was a vision. And I hope that it will be fulfilled. And it is related to what I just told you. Uh, I thought that if all the mothers of the whole world would unite, they would be able to stop wars, not by sitting and drinking coffee but being active and doing things and only this thing-- just saving the children from war. So here is the poem, 'The Mother's Song.' 'From me he springs.' Oh, I am the mother. 'From me, he springs, the source of joy and sufferings. I, the mother, guard his way, from his birth till my last day. I am the mother with a heart and blood.

speaker: interviewer
text: I make him a man that loves and builds, creates and fashions, and sees beyond. I am the mother. He is my son. How can I keep my peace always while cries of horror turn my heart, when he stormed from me to war and homeward he will come no more? An end to silence and to tears, I say to him, while my life endures.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll call aloud to every nation, to every mother of every man, lift up your hand. Men's blood is one. Do not despair. We'll save him yet. A living wall of all men's mothers, brown and white and black, a towering wall which does not lack the yellow and the red. Over every clash, a sense what is in common and transcends. Millions, differing in all, yet share the same great happiness. To have a son. To be the mother and call a living son by name. And millions will rise. Millions of hands. And they will be uplifted into the sky in an oath. Our sons to slaughter we will not give. Earth be our witness and heavens above.'

speaker: interviewer
text: When you met Otto, where was that and when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was in 1945 in Jerusalem in a coffee shop-- Cafe Atara. And, uh, I loved him from the very first moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see when you saw him that first time?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, he was so handsome. And then he was so clever. I never met anybody who was that clever. And we would go. He would take me home, a few miles-- not by bus. And we were walking and walking. And he was talking and explaining and asking and listening. He was fantastic.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 21.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was Otto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Otto was 45. And my mother was not too happy about the difference in age. But I never felt it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it about him that separated those 24 years?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean, about him?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was there no difference between those 24 years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he was so much interested in what I was doing, in what I was thinking and feeling. It was like one unit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us more about Otto Schneid? Where was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in Czechoslovakia in a little town called Jablunkova, in-- and the date was the 30th of January, 1900.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was he from Czechoslovakia? Was his family from Czechoslovakia originally?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. His family was not from Czechoslovakia. They have a Polish background. But, uh, he, himself-- his father was working for the courts. And he was working in Jablunkova. That's why they were there. But afterwards, they went to Bielitz, which is-- which became afterwards Poland. And there, he spent most of his life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were his parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: His parents-- father is Yaakov, and his parents-- mother is, uh, Branislava, in Hebrew, Bluma.

speaker: interviewer
text: That is his mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His mother's. And his mother was-- her maiden name is [? Pelkan. ?] His mother came from a family of farmers. Her father was a farmer, great-grandfather was a farmer. And people with not too much education, but when Yaakov, the father of, uh, of Otto, came there and saw her, she was so beautiful that he fell in love with her in spite of the difference-- the cultural differences that were between them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of background is his paternal side?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, his fraternal side is, uh, very distinguished. His great-grandfather was Shir, Shlomo Yehuda Rapoport, who is, from one of the encyclopedias, I have a few words about him. Um, he was described as first in [NON-ENGLISH], and first in [NON-ENGLISH], the wisdom of Israel. Translated-- knew many languages. And he, Shir himself, married the daughter of [? Ketzot ?] [? Hachoshen. ?] So you have all these great families on both sides. And the daughter of Shir, [? Gietel, ?] married Dr. Otto Schneid, who was the grandfather of Otto. It was-- he was a doctor for the poor. He, uh, went over to the poor people who invited him. And [? Gietel ?] prepared for him some chicken to bring them. And money, he didn't take from them. So he was really a very poor man. But he did good all his life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was Otto educated in his upbringing? What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, he studied in a gymnasium, where they taught also Hebrew. And, uh, Berkowitz, who was the secretary of Herzl, was his teacher. Berkowitz didn't succeed telling-- teaching Herzl Hebrew, but he succeeded teaching Otto. When Otto came to Israel, uh, he spoke perfectly Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other languages was Otto taught?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, Greek, Latin, French, of course, German. Um, he knew Italian. He knew a lot of languages. Wherever he needed a language, it was at his disposal.

speaker: interviewer
text: In addition to a public education, did he receive a private Jewish education, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He didn't get it privately. But in his gymnasium was the Hasmonea, which was an organiza-- a Zionist organization of youth. And, uh, he was a member of the Hasmonea there. And then he left to Vienna to continue his studies in the University of Vienna. And, uh, at the end, he got his PhD there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What subject matters had he studied to get his doctorate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, first of all, his-- he-- his parents wanted him to be a doctor. So he took one year of medicine. But he couldn't stand the-- the blood and all that. He was very sensitive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after a year, he studied-- took another year, studied, uh, pharmacy. And it was very boring for him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he went to history of art and the aesthetics and the art of the Chinese people and the art all over the world. And he was writing books.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he also paint and sculpt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He painted. May-- we don't have paintings from that period when he was in Poland because, uh, I will tell you later how he left Poland. He didn't carry with him anything. But he was always drawing. Always drawing, and we have lots of draw-- old drawings that he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he had a sister, Genia Hamburger. And, uh, she studied to be a teacher. But she didn't work after she married Ham-- Hugo Hamburger. Both of them were lost in the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was Hugo from? Where was Hugo from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am not sure. I think that he was from Kattowitz, but I am not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Otto have any hobbies or particular activities that he was fond of?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the activities was a very dangerous one. This was climbing mountains. And he went on the Tatra Mountains, and he went on the Alps. I think that it brought lots of aggravation to his parents because they were worried. And I have here a story that he wrote. And that story is describing one night in the Tatras, when he was almost dead. But he decided that he's going to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'll tell you very briefly about that story. He went with a friend, Maurice [? Zieberschtein. ?] And, uh, both of them planned to go on the Tatras for three days and took with them equipment-- whatever they needed for three days. But they thought that it's not going to be a risky trip.

speaker: interviewee
text: When they came to the Tatras, suddenly, Otto says that he looked. And he saw something like a gutter or like a chimney. And it's very easy to climb there because you can use both sides of the chimney. So he said to Maurice, wait here, and I'll climb. Suddenly, he was climbing fast. And suddenly, he saw that there was a block of stone. And that block was very hard to overcome and to climb on top of it. But he did that. And he said to Maurice, I will pull you up. Come. Throw him the rope, and, uh, Maurice was pulled up. Fine and dandy.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was almost evening. The last rays of the sun. What are doing, responsible people? They would say, OK, let us sleep here overnight. And in the morning, we will continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: But Otto said, perhaps it is very close to the peak. Let's continue a little bit more. And Maurice said, I'll stay here. You go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went, and it was terribly hard, till he found himself hanging on one hand, and water trickling-- cold, icy water trickling down his arm, without no way to move up or down. And right there, when he was sitting-- uh, when he was hanging like that-- like there, Maurice asked him, why are you so quiet? What happened? And Otto told him briefly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Maurice said, I'll call for help. So he called for help, help. And the mountains were echoing the help.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was an answer. Three people were coming to help. How many are you? Is one injured? Where are you located? But Maurice didn't wait for, where are you located? He already started to burn his handkerchief and the-- and his linen. And they knew where it is.

speaker: interviewee
text: And- and Otto is holding still to this piece of rock. And, uh, they are coming. They are coming, and he can hear them, that he's-- that they are putting pegs in the stones, till they came to Maurice. And he hears them talking to Maurice. But suddenly, they turn around and go away. So that was a terrible moment. And I will read you just a little from that story, 'The Night I Decided to Live.' 'So I was in most remote loneliness. From time to time, my friend called something. I replied, but I didn't torment him with questions. The hand that bore me seemed to have become a part of the rock. Then it became, again, a living, human limb, imploring me for permission to loosen up. Now, however, when I was so abandoned, I understood that the very final decision was up to me. The fingers could run out of their ability only after my will would-- would cancel itself. My hand could really go loose only by my own decision of suicide.' And he decided he's going to hold onto the rope no matter what, no matter how long. He will live. He doesn't want to break the heart of his father and mother and perhaps a beloved girl. And he doesn't want to kill Maurice who is standing underneath. After a long time, he heard, again, voices. He thought he wa-- he was dreaming. And they were coming from above. They couldn't climb the way he did. So they took the long road and went above the mountain. And from there, they came, and they picked him up. And they put a rope around him, and he was saved.

speaker: interviewer
text: The end of this story is that it is very strange. When they came back home, Maurice's mother, who was a widow and not too well educated, told him, Maurice, you know what I dreamt tonight? And she described the whole situation of how Otto and he were in the mountains-- lost in the mountains.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when he was 24, after his father's death, he promised his mother that he will never climb the mountains again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about how his father died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. His father got cancer at the age of 57. And, uh, Otto had bad feelings about it because there was a doctor-- a famous doctor. But he was already old. And they wanted that this doctor would operate on-- on his father. And the doctor said, I will-- I will participate. So this was not enough for Otto. And he went to another doctor, and the other doctor was ready to operate, of course. And, uh, after a short while, he-- he had two good weeks. But after a short while, he, uh, died.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he met that professor, that doctor-- the first one. And the doctor asked him, how is your father doing? He said, he's dead. Why didn't you bring him to me? Oh, because you said that you are only going to intervene, and you are not going to do that. I meant I'm going to do that. So can you imagine the feelings that he had afterwards-- the guilt feelings?

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was cancer. And, uh, Otto himself was afraid all his life that at the age of 57 he will probably also die like his father. And he died from cancer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- do you know the name of the first doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember the names.

speaker: interviewer
text: After that, uh, his mother was the head of the household. How did she support the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was very, very hard for her to support the family. She rented the place in the mountains. And she had what they call a pensione-- a pensione, a place where people would come and enjoy and eat and so on. And she was cooking all the time, preparing for them. And Genia and Hugo moved from Kattowitz and stayed with the mother to help her out. And Otto would come from time to time while he was not traveling to help out and to be with h-- with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what village this was in the mountains?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, I don't remember. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when Otto was at school, are there any incidents that you can recall him telling you of any anti-Semitism that he experienced?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the university, when he came to the university in Vienna, he saw a gathering of students. And one of them was standing and speaking against the Jews. And he was screaming and speaking. And Otto just arrived. He didn't know anybody there. But when he heard that, he went over to this student. He pick-- he was a very strong man. He picked him up, and with the student, he hit all the crowds so that [LAUGHS] they run away from him. This was the-- one of the stories that he told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: But Otto, in general, was not a violent man, would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was not a violent man at all. But if you touched Jewish people, that's a different story.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe his political leanings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, besides being a Zionist, he was also a pacifist. And he was worried about the world and the atom bomb and the armament and all that. He tried whatever he could in every way to speak to this or that and write and so on in order to stop this craziness. And one thing that he wrote afterwards, here in Toronto already, was a book called because this is really what he was doing-- searching for rescue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, let's talk about Otto's career as an art historian. In 1930, what did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, before 1930, he was traveling all over and delivering lectures. And then he wrote a book, [NON-ENGLISH]. And then in 1930, he decided that he's going to write a book, like an encyclopedia about Jewish artists. Uh, that would have to be done by going to the studios of the artists and taking photographs and so on. And he started it in 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And after that, Hitler came to power. And when Hitler came to power and declared the Jewish artists decadent art, it was again like that student talking to the crowd and telling them how terrible Jews are. Whenever there had-- there was something that had to be done, Otto was the one who felt that he has to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he decided that he's going to change his idea of an encyclopedia. He's going to write a very objective book about Jewish art so that Hitler would be able to read it and understand really what Jewish art is and that it is not decadent. And he worked on that book till 1938.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1938, the book-- no, I didn't tell you how he got his photographs. When he came to an artist and he said, I am going to write about you a chapter, most of the artists-- and I know for sure about Chagall-- said to him, Otto, take a little painting, just a little painting. And he would always say, I am sorry. I cannot take any paintings. My hands have to be clean. But what he took from them were photographs. He needed the photographs. He had to make sure that he will remember what he saw. And he collected these photographs. The photo-- 160 photographs were with that book, with a preface by Martin Buber in, uh, in Vienna. And the Nazis came in. They confiscated the book. They saw exactly what Otto planned to do, and they put him on the black list. Otto was at the time in Warsaw. And he knew that he is on the black list, and he knew that the book was confiscated. And he knew that the Polish government is going to find him and to send him back to his homeland, to Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, Austria was--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- he was an Austrian.

speaker: interviewer
text: How's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, when he was in Austria, when he studied, uh, he became a citizen of Austria. So he belongs to Austria. But he knew that in Austria will not be his homeland but his home of death.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was hiding for about six months. And at the end of that, he fell into the hands of the police-- the Polish police. And he was put in prison because he wouldn't reveal to them where did he hide during these six months. Now, when you say, he fell into the hands of the Polish police--

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody told him that he will be able to, uh, to leave Poland if he will go directly to the police and be honest with them and so on. So he thought, perhaps. And he went there, and they put him into prison. And he was in prison three days, 1938. Actually, it's beginning of 1939. All the Jewish community in Warsaw was up in arms. Otto Schneid is in prison. And they did the unbelievable thing. They got for him a research student certificate from the Hebrew University.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that book that fell into the hands of the Nazis helped him to save his life. If not that book, he would be killed. He would stay in Europe. So the end of it was that, uh, he had another day-- just one day-- to see his mother and the family. And then he went to Israel. We're going to stop here and go to the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. How does--
